ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper advances a notable and conceptually distinct perspective by casting sensorimotor control as supervised prediction of future intrinsic measurements conditioned on observation, action and a parameterized goal, rather than optimizing scalar rewards via standard RL. The use of vectorial, temporally dense measurement prediction and goal-conditioning to select actions is a non-trivial reframing that is clearly differentiated from DQN/A3C/DSR baselines and is empirically validated (including a competition win). From my theory-oriented viewpoint this is a solid novel methodological contribution, albeit largely empirical and reframing in nature (no formal guarantees presented and limitations like reactivity and discrete actions remain), so it stops short of being a breakthrough theoretical innovation. | B(4): The paper reframes sensorimotor control as supervised learning of future intrinsic measurements (DFP) rather than conventional scalar-reward RL, which is a conceptually clear and practically useful new perspective that led to strong empirical gains (outperforming DQN/A3C/DSR and winning the Visual Doom AI Competition). The contribution is well differentiated from prior work (explicit prediction network F(o_t,a,g;θ) regressing vectorial future measurements at multiple horizons) and represents a non‑trivial methodological departure from standard value- or policy‑based methods. From an applied RL viewpoint this would surprise and inform practitioners by showing dense, multi-dimensional measurement prediction improves generalization and training speed, though it is not a purely theoretical breakthrough and builds on existing predictive/control ideas.

From an applied RL viewpoint this would surprise and inform practitioners by showing dense, multi-dimensional measurement prediction improves generalization and training speed, though it is not a purely theoretical breakthrough and builds on existing predictive/control ideas.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear progression from method (DFP) to experiments on multiple ViZDoom scenarios and a concise conclusion; the main contributions are stated early and aligned with the experimental claims (including competition win). Notation for the predictive model F(o_t,a,g;θ) and the measurement-vector formulation is presented and consistent in the summary, and figures/tables (task suite, baselines, metrics) seem effective and informative. Writing quality is reported as good with thorough related work coverage and hyperparameter reporting, but there are minor presentation gaps (e.g., no mention of code availability and some limits—reactive model, discrete actions—could be highlighted earlier) that prevent an exceptional rating. | B(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from motivation through method and experiments, with the main contributions stated early (supervised future-prediction formulation, DFP model, and competition win). Notation and methodology are presented consistently and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (tasks, baselines, metrics) effectively support the empirical claims on ViZDoom benchmarks. A few sections (e.g., limitations about memory and continuous actions) are tersely stated and could benefit from slightly more exposition for reproducibility (code is not mentioned), but overall the writing is clear and free of major ambiguity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a clear progression from method (DFP) to experiments on multiple ViZDoom scenarios and a concise conclusion; the main contributions are stated early and aligned with the experimental claims (including competition win). Notation for the predictive model F(o_t,a,g;θ) and the measurement-vector formulation is presented and consistent in the summary, and figures/tables (task suite, baselines, metrics) seem effective and informative. Writing quality is reported as good with thorough related work coverage and hyperparameter reporting, but there are minor presentation gaps (e.g., no mention of code availability and some limits—reactive model, discrete actions—could be highlighted earlier) that prevent an exceptional rating.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from motivation through method and experiments, with the main contributions stated early (supervised future-prediction formulation, DFP model, and competition win). Notation and methodology are presented consistently and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (tasks, baselines, metrics) effectively support the empirical claims on ViZDoom benchmarks. A few sections (e.g., limitations about memory and continuous actions) are tersely stated and could benefit from slightly more exposition for reproducibility (code is not mentioned), but overall the writing is clear and free of major ambiguity.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper offers strong empirical evidence (multiple ViZDoom scenarios, comparisons to baselines, and competition performance) that supports its central claims. However, they also note the absence of formal theoretical analysis, limited model scope (no memory or continuous actions), and some reproducibility/robustness gaps. Given the convincing experiments but remaining questions about generality and theoretical grounding, a score of 4 reflects solid empirical soundness with notable but not fatal limitations.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides substantial empirical evidence (multiple ViZDoom scenarios, comparisons to DQN/A3C/DSR, and competition win) that its supervised future-measurement prediction approach improves performance, so the experimental claims are plausibly supported. However, from a theory-focused perspective there is no formal analysis or guarantees explaining when or why the method should work, and the summary indicates the model is purely reactive (no memory) and limited to discrete actions—important modeling assumptions that are not theoretically justified. It is also unclear whether ablation studies and exhaustive robustness analyses (sensitivity to hyperparameters, distribution shifts, more classical baselines) are sufficiently comprehensive, leaving logical gaps about failure modes and generality. Overall the work is empirically strong but lacks the theoretical grounding and robustness analysis I require for a higher technical score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s central claims are clearly articulated and supported by extensive empirical evidence: DFP is evaluated across multiple ViZDoom scenarios (D1–D4 and multitexture variants), compared to relevant baselines (DQN, A3C, DSR), shows faster training and superior task performance (including a >50% lead in the Visual Doom AI Competition), and reports hyperparameters. However, there are no formal proofs (the contribution is algorithmic/empirical), and the method is presented without memory or continuous-action extensions and with no mention of released code or statistical significance testing—minor reproducibility and generality concerns that don’t overturn the experimental conclusions.

main_claims: Sensorimotor control can be formulated as supervised learning by predicting future intrinsic measurements conditioned on current observation, action, and a parameterized goal.

A deep network trained to directly predict future measurements (DFP) outperforms state-of-the-art deep RL methods (DQN, A3C, DSR) on challenging 3D Doom-based tasks, especially when multiple measurements are available.
Training with vectorial, temporally dense measurements yields better performance and generalization across environments and goals than using a scalar reward.
A model trained with this approach won the Full Deathmatch track of the Visual Doom AI Competition, outperforming the second best submission by more than 50%.
_summary.methodology: Train a deep network F(ot,a,g;θ) to regress future differences of designated measurement vectors at multiple temporal offsets given current sensory input, measurements, action and a parameterized goal; at test time choose the action that maximizes the goal-weighted predicted future measurements.
datasets: ViZDoom (Doom scenarios: D1 Basic, D2 Navigation, D3 Battle, D4 Battle 2; multitexture variants D3-tx, D4-tx)
metrics: average health at end of episode
average frags at end of episode
training speed (million simulation steps per day)
limitations: Model is purely reactive (no explicit memory) as presented.
Model was developed for discrete action spaces; continuous actions not addressed.
Predicting full sensory input (rather than designated measurements) is stated as beyond current capabilities.
_summary.topic_area: Reinforcement learning / sensorimotor control via supervised future prediction in immersive 3D environments

reviewer_a.name: Theory-focused Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in machine learning with a track record of theoretical work on control and learning; has published analyses of sample complexity and formal guarantees for RL algorithms.
traits: Penalizes papers that present mainly empirical results without accompanying theoretical analysis (e.g., no proofs, bounds, or principled characterization of when the method should work) — will deduct points if there is no theoretical justification.
Requires comprehensive ablations and at least three strong baselines including classical/analytical methods; will penalize insufficient comparison or incomplete ablation studies.
Demands analysis of failure modes and robustness (e.g., sensitivity to hyperparameters, distributions shifts); deducts points if such analysis is missing.
Prioritizes conceptual novelty of the objective/learning principle (not just engineering of network/benchmarks) and will downscore if the contribution is largely incremental empirical engineering.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Critical, focused on formal grounding and explanatory analysis — rewards principled novelty and penalizes empiricism without theory.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Empiricalist
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in applied reinforcement learning with experience organizing/participating in RL competitions and deploying agents in complex 3D environments.
traits: Strongly rewards clear, extensive empirical evaluation that demonstrates consistent, reproducible performance gains across diverse tasks and metrics; will give higher scores if results beat state-of-the-art and include competition wins.

Values novel problem formulations and pragmatic algorithmic solutions (e.g., reframing control as supervised prediction); will upscore papers that introduce useful, simple paradigms even without formal guarantees.

Prefers methods that show good generalization across environments/goals and use dense vectorial signals rather than sparse scalar rewards; will penalize if experiments are narrow or results do not transfer.
Favors reproducibility: gives credit if implementation details, hyperparameters, and competition-level evaluation are thorough; deducts points if key experimental details are missing.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Empirically driven and application-oriented — rewards practical impact, strong benchmarks, and reproducibility over formal proofs.
